codings.evidence_note: DQN compared against GA, PSO, and traditional Rule-Based scheduling on a Python simulation platform (multiple metaheuristics + a rule, but no exact solver or prior learned method); text states 'The cost of the RL model is 20% lower than that of the GA model' (strongest comparator, at 150 tasks), with a weighted multi-objective reward penalizing resource shortage/delay/cost/quality violations.

PPO on a disjunctive-graph FRCPSP compared against priority rules (FIFO, SOT, SD, PR), a GA metaheuristic, and a prior learned RL method (FC-PPO); 'achieves the shortest average PD of 14.40 h, representing a 8.63% reduction compared with the best competing method (FC-PPO)'; invalid-action logits set to negative infinity (action mask).

PPO labor-hiring agent compared only against naive fixed strategies (daily-labor-only and monthly-labor-only) in a simulation calibrated on a real 25-month Thai project; 'achieved up to 11.5% and 23.4% cost reductions compared to baseline strategies using only daily or only monthly labor'; strongest comparator (daily-only) gives 11.5%; reward includes penalties for schedule overruns and over-hiring.

PPO actor-critic with task-level action masking over a DAG of task dependencies+resources, compared against CPM, Genetic Algorithm, and Earliest Start (multiple heuristics + a metaheuristic, no exact solver or prior learned method); '20.3% improvement over the Critical Path Method (CPM)' in duration and '43.6% reduction in total CO2' vs CPM/GA/ES.

Graph-attention RL for RCPSP compared against CPM-Heuristic, an earliest-start Priority Rule, and MLP-RL (a prior flattened-state learned method), on synthetic instances; only makespan values are tabulated (no explicit percentage over the strongest comparator MLP-RL is stated), and masked softmax gives zero probability to infeasible tasks.

PPO with constraint-based invalid action masking for repetitive-project scheduling under soft logic and resource constraints, stated to 'outperform traditional exact and metaheuristic methods in solution quality' (exact CP/MILP + metaheuristics, no priority-rule or prior-learned baseline shown); excerpt is truncated before results so no numeric percentage is extractable; objective is minimizing project duration.

DRL policy 'evaluated against a priority-based rule-of-thumb dispatch baseline' (single untuned rule) and 'improves the average production rate per truck cycle by approximately 17%'; evaluated on a discrete-event-simulation digital twin of the Sungun mine.

A3C/POMDP agent compared to a single traditional fixed maintenance policy (NDT every two years, replace at state 4): 'AI can save 27.8% cost compared to the traditional maintenance policy' (abstract range 5-30%), evaluated via Monte-Carlo case studies; the five maintenance actions are always selectable (constraint-free).

PPO agent 'lowered the total amount of project delays by 18 percent compared to those associated with fixed-schedule baselines' in an OpenAI-Gym simulation; reward is an explicitly weighted time/cost/safety combination (multi-objective).

Q-learning-guided Mountain Gazelle metaheuristic compared against multiple metaheuristics (NDSII-MGO/Jaya/TLBO, MOGA, NDSII-Rao-2); primary metric is hypervolume (0.945 vs 0.943 for NDSII-Rao-2, non-significant), no clean percentage over the strongest comparator on the multi-objective primary.

CONSTRUCTA compared only against its own GPT-4o 'Basic Context' ablation ('sampling random rows as context'), reporting '+28.9% in automated planning'; no scheduling baseline (no rule, metaheuristic, solver, or prior learned method), experiments on proprietary data.

GAPS-RL compared to static CPM, SVR, LSTM and Reactive RL (a prior learned method, multiple baselines but no exact/metaheuristic solver); 'GAPS-RL achieved improvements of approximately 8.3% in completion time, 8.5% in total cost, and 7.9% in resource utilization compared to reactive RL' on 500 synthetic road-project DAGs.

DDQN compared against multiple GA-based optimization runs (a standard metaheuristic) plus DRL-architecture variants; 'achieves a more robust and superior LoR by 6% with four times less training time compared to the best GA-based outcome.' Feasibility: 'adopting an action mask to penalize invalid repair actions.' Post-disaster substation repair sequencing, resilience/recovery-time (LoR) objective; evaluated entirely in a simulated substation environment.

GCN-DRL (S1) compared with four conventional methods: two greedy rules (S2,S3), a GA (S4, metaheuristic), and diameter-ranking (S5). Table 3 (scenario 1): SRI S1=41.67 vs strongest comparator S3=39.225 => ~6.23% (study reports SRI values, not a percentage). Action = pick a not-yet-repaired pipe from the damaged set (Eqs 13-14 forbid repeats), so all actions feasible by construction; SRI/recovery-time objective, testbed WDN simulation.

Actor-Critic DQN maintenance policy compared to 'the most effective traditional maintenance policy... a reactive strategy, where repairs are conducted once the structure's condition level falls to State Level 2' (single threshold rule); 'the trained optimal policy shows an improvement of nearly 10% in each sustainability metric.' Reward R = wenv*uenv + wecon*uecon + wsafe*usafe (explicitly weighted multi-objective); bridge-network life-cycle maintenance, simulated MDP.

SAC schedule compared against the original field/manual progressive-tamping execution plan (single empirical strategy) and against other RL algorithms (TD3, PPO, PPO-E, A2C); no priority-rule suite, metaheuristic, or exact solver. 'total annual budget... CNY 2,463,440, which is CNY 236,260 lower than the original plan... reduction of approximately 8.75%.' Return action 'enforced through the action mask'; state-dependent reward weights condition/cost/failure (w1-w5). Case study on a real 50 km track with Gamma-process simulation.

Only comparator is vanilla Q-learning ('comparing the reinforcement learning decision model... with the Q-learning algorithm'); no priority dispatching rule, metaheuristic, or exact solver. Reported figures are learning-algorithm metrics (abstract: 60% faster convergence, 20% higher average reward; conclusion inverts these to 20%/60%) and internally inconsistent, with no percentage gain on the primary benefit-cost/cost objective over a scheduling baseline. PPO-with-Clip pavement maintenance, LTPP-data simulation.

SNO (QMIX MARL) compared only against the previously built TSBU (TD3-Wolpertinger) learned model; no priority rule, metaheuristic, or exact solver baseline. 'compared to TSBU, SNO produced a 3.0% reduction in total costs and an average pavement performance improvement of up to 17.5%'; primary objective is minimized total discounted cost. Budget overrun handled via penalty term (penalty factor p); vertical constraint via added overlay cost. Real-scale Jiangsu highway network, simulation with traffic-assignment/BNN performance models.

Table 4 compares the TSG-GNN-DLSA method against Random, DLS (fixed priority rule), SA, scalarized GA, and NSGA-II metaheuristics (no exact solver or prior learned method), so baseline strength is 2; the headline 19.69-pp RU gain (68.24->87.93%) is versus the random baseline, and no percentage improvement over the strongest comparator NSGA-II (81.67%) is explicitly reported.

Conclusion states path prediction accuracy 'improved by 6.85%' (95.38% vs strongest baseline LSTM 88.53%, also CPM 76.42% and PERT 78.91%); reward integrates weighted time, cost, and quality objectives; evaluated on BuildingNet dataset in simulation.

Only baseline is a single Greedy heuristic (rubric 1); framework 'achieves 100% task completion under moderately stressed conditions, whereas a Greedy baseline drops to completion rates as low as 80%,' but no clean percentage improvement over the Greedy comparator on a primary objective is reported; MaskablePPO enforces feasibility via RAG-derived action-masking rules in a simulated prefab supply-chain environment.

Compares against tuned/rule-based management, deterministic optimization and rolling-horizon MPC/stochastic-robust optimization (solver-class), and multiple prior learned methods (single-agent DRL, MAPPO, MADDPG/MATD3, QMIX, HAPPO), meeting rubric 3; headline 18.7% cost-overrun reduction is relative to rule-based management (not the strongest comparator), and no percentage over the strongest learned baseline is given; hierarchical reward penalizes cost/delay/safety violations in a calibrated simulation.

Sole comparator is the NSGA-II metaheuristic across CP-MP settings (rubric 2); results report the PPO+MOEA/D hypervolume was 'approximately 5% greater than that of NSGA-II' with higher hypervolumes in 95% of scenarios; hard constraints (precedence/resources) enforced via action masking, evaluated on a real-world project in simulation.

Compared against three handcrafted heuristics (Heur6, Cheapest-Feasible, FastestETA); versus the strongest baseline (Cheapest-Feasible/FastestETA) DRQL reduces total cost by 9.01% (Table 3), with delay -10.62%; feasibility enforced by indicator action masking, trained purely in silico.

DRL (Q-learning) benchmarked against Monte Carlo simulation and a genetic algorithm (a standard metaheuristic); it 'reduced delay by 33.6% (versus the Monte Carlo simulation) and 46.4% (versus the GA)', so 33.6% is the gain over the stronger comparator on the time parameter; evaluated in a virtual simulation environment and formulated as an RCMPSP; material-availability filtering eliminates infeasible module-type allocations; objectives (time, cost, social vulnerability) equally weighted.

RL dynamic scheduling compared only against a single static Critical Path Method schedule: 'The dynamic schedule reduced average delays by 47% and cost overruns by 58% compared to the static schedule'; tested on historical datasets (simulation), no feasibility mechanism described.

ANN policy trained with RL uses 'a rule-of-thumb policy ... as a benchmark for comparison' (single untuned rule); optimized policy reached 168.9 time-units improvement per component, 'approximately 34.4% of the expected production time', measured against the rule-of-thumb; online dispatch of PRC components arriving as a Poisson stream (no activity network) in a simulated factory.

DQN-EA-SR (distributed flexible job-shop, precast concrete) compared against two metaheuristics (EMA, HMOMA) and three prior learned methods (SLGA, DQN-L, HDQN) but no standalone tuned-rule or exact-solver baseline, so it misses the level-3 'tuned rules' conjunct; on HV it 'performs well 27.9%, 32.8%, 16.3%, 34.6% and 41.2% better than EMA, HMOMA, SLGA, DQN-L, and HDQN', i.e. 16.3% over the strongest comparator (SLGA); TWET and TEC minimized simultaneously (HV/IGD).

No algorithmic scheduling baseline; the CC model's revised lookahead plans are compared only to the original human plans on two case studies 'simulated from an actual construction project', reducing duration by 40 days (of 216) and 104 days (of 320) with no percentage reported; infeasible (cyclic/inconsistent) assignments penalized via a large constant cost of 10000 in the A* consistency term.

Each module beats only a single weak traditional baseline (CPM for scheduling, one Rule-Based system for resource allocation at 68% vs DQN-GA 87% in Table 3, parametric for cost); stronger comparators actually present (pure DQN 82%, XGBoost 8% deviation) receive no reported percentage gain, so headline figures (27.9% utilization, 63.6% deviation) are only vs the weakest comparators.

Framework compared against CPM, PERT, heuristic resource levelling, pure MILP (exact/feasible-but-not-adaptive), and pure RL; replicated case study reports 'make span cut by 10% over MILP alone' (residential) with RL outputs verified/enforced by MILP feasibility check.

Accessible text ends before Section 4 results; abstract states the A3C e-Maintenance system 'significantly enhances the overall performance ... across multiple lifecycle indicators' (condition, serviceability, risk, sustainability) over a 100-year simulated service period, but names no experimental baseline and gives no numeric comparison.

IRLCOF compared against five tuned metaheuristic/RL algorithms (BCSS, KDMA, MAPSO-QLS, BOIG, QLMHHA) and the CPLEX exact solver; abstract states 'IRLCOF is at least 20% better than other comparison algorithms ... in the Inverse Generational Distance metric' for the carbon-efficient no-wait flow shop (makespan + total carbon emissions).

High-level MAPPO policy compared against a priority-based method and a genetic algorithm; policy averages 30,000 steps/episode vs priority method 33,000 ('10% improvement compared to the baseline methods') and GA 35,000, with a construction-knowledge decision table restricting each agent's target set to feasible executable tasks.

Multi-task PopArt-A3C benchmarked against GA (metaheuristic) plus DDPG, PPO and plain A3C (prior learned DRL) on a real Sichuan earthwork DES; 'generated schemes reduce total transportation time by 24.1% compared to traditional methods.'

Compared only against 'the most effective traditional condition-based maintenance strategy' (a single heuristic policy, rubric 1), improving environmental/economic/safety metrics by 31.91%/47.05%/38.02%; equally-weighted multi-attribute utility (multi-objective) so no single primary-objective gain, and budget enforced via the reward's max-utility term (penalty). Evaluated on an existing 308-bridge network in a MDP simulation.

Case study 3 benchmarks RL-ABM against priority rules MITF(74)/SAD(71)/MILFT(67) plus metaheuristics GA and PSO; RL-ABM reaches 64 days, matching the strongest comparators GA/PSO (0% gain over them) while the paper's headline 15% duration reduction is over the resource-constrained CPM baseline. Agent selects only from 'Ready' activities whose predecessors and resources are satisfied (masking); literature-derived case studies (simulation).

Compared vs CPM, CPM+manual, and GA metaheuristic; real case gives RL 111 days vs prior GA (Dong et al.) 112 days, but across the synthetic larger experiments GA yields shorter duration in S1/S2/S5 while RL wins at larger scale, so no single clean duration gain over the strongest comparator (GA); the reported headline advantage is ~300x faster runtime, and constraint violations are counted inside the reward (penalty).

Benchmarked against the OR-Tools solver, GA metaheuristic, AND prior learned attention models (AM/SAM) for the same routing problem; text states DAM (UHCRP20-trained) solving UHCRP100 is 'significantly lower by 29.75% and 41.42% compared to OR-Tools and GA' on expected failure loss (objective = risk/other). Only unvisited feasible nodes are selectable (masking); wind-farm 'real-world' case is a computational study on real turbine coordinates (simulation).

Human-in-the-loop DRL module 'reduced overtime by approximately 6%' (91 h) with 75% supervisor adoption over nine months of field deployment on a real Texas mid-rise project, but no algorithmic comparator is run for the DRL (improvement is over project status quo, rubric 0); feasibility handling not described (NR).

MAS-with-smart-agent (plan 1) compared against the prior conventional tuned-priority-rule MAS (plan 2) and DES: plan 1 finishes 71 time steps (3.1%) earlier than conventional MAS and 13.8% faster than DES, with crew walking distance cut 6-10%. Crew agents may select only tasks whose precedence constraints are satisfied (masking); on-site precast wall-component assembly at a real Shanghai building, run as an agent simulation.

Abstract: 'reduced the operational cost by 15.72, and 24.80% compared against Solver, and metaheuristic methods, such [as] differential evolution' (PSO also used); strongest comparator (Solver) = 15.72%; no priority rules or prior learned method compared.

Only baseline is the historical project average: DQN scheduling 'increased [equipment utilization] from the historical project average of 68% to 89% (during the peak construction period)'; no designed comparator and no reported percentage improvement on a scheduling objective; applied on a real Guizhou PV project.

Tables compare RL only against CPM and PERT (duration 165 vs 180/175 days, cost 570 vs 600/590, utilization 92% vs 85/87%) using hand-computed illustrative numbers; multi-objective with no single clean percentage over a strongest comparator; constraint violations handled via penalty mechanism on a simulation platform.

DQN-optimized plans vs manual expert plans and vs Q-learning (prior learned): Plan 1 saves 80 days (6.68%) vs manual Plan 3, and the best DQN plan (Plan 2) beats the strongest manual baseline Plan 4 by 40 days (3.49%); ILP/Gurobi used internally, not as a competing solver baseline; case study on real HSR tunnel data; dual-objective (duration + number of teams).

Abstract/results: IML yields 'average improvement of about 8.66% and 13.78%' over two recently published algorithms; also compared against LPT/SPT/NEH heuristics, QIG/MPGA/VNS metaheuristics, and prior graph-based IL; strongest comparator = 8.66%; decoder masks already-scheduled jobs (logits set to -inf) to minimize makespan.

Reports 'approximate 5% reduction in total project cost' vs priority-rule/two-stage heuristic-PR methods (case-study gaps 4.92%/5.89%); compared against MARL (MAPPO/QMIX/VDN) and 4 priority rules but no exact/metaheuristic solver; gap vs strongest RL comparator (MAPPO/QMIX) is only ~2-3% in the tables; objective J is weighted time+cost.
Case Study 3 DQN task allocation, +32% productivity vs Traditional Method baseline; no rules/solver/prior-learned comparator.
